sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.

Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 87 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Excellent! Key finding: When left square is at col 9 (not col 19), UP moves the RIGHT square up instead!
This is a crucial discovery. The semantics are:
- When left square is at col 19: it's at row 19, right square at row 54. Left is higher, so UP moves left up (blocked).
- When left square is at col 9: it's at row 19, right square at row 54. But UP moved the RIGHT square up from 54 to 49, then 44, then 39!
This means the vertical comparison for UP/DOWN is NOT based on the actual row values, but on some other criterion. Or maybe when the left square is at col 9, its row is treated differently.
Wait, let me re-examine. After LEFT x3:
- Left: (19, 9) - boundary [[19, 9], [19, 13], [23, 13], [23, 9]]
- Right: (54, 44) - boundary [[54, 44], [54, 48], [58, 48], [58, 44]]
Then UP moved right from 54 to 49 to 44 to 39. So UP moved the RIGHT square, not the left square.
This contradicts my earlier understanding. Let me re-examine the earlier evidence:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39 (both moved up)
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34 (both moved up)
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29 (both moved up)
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (only left moved)
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39 (only left moved)
In these cases, left was at col 19 and right was at col 34. When left was at row 29 and right at row 39, left was higher (29 < 39), and UP moved left up.
But now, left is at row 19, col 9, and right is at row 54, col 44. Left is higher (19 < 54), but UP moved right up.
The difference is the column positions. When left is at col 9 and right is at col 44, UP moves the right square. When left is at col 19 and right is at col 34, UP moves the left square.
Maybe the rule is: UP moves the square that is in the LEFT chamber (or the one at the lower column)? No, that doesn't make sense either.
Wait, let me think again. Maybe the rule is based on which square can move up, not on which is higher. If the left square at col 9 is blocked from moving up, then UP moves the right square instead.
Let me check: left at col 9, row 19. Can it move up? The boundary shows [19,9] to [19,13], which is at row 19. So the top of the chamber at col 9 is row 19. The square can't move up from row 19 at col 9.
So the rule might be: UP moves the square that can move up. If both can, both move. If only one can, that one moves.
This would explain:
- Left at col 19, row 19: can't move up (blocked at row 19). Right at col 34, row 39: can move up. But earlier, UP didn't move the right square when left was at row 19, col 19.
Hmm, that contradicts. Let me re-examine. Earlier, left was at row 19, col 19, and right was at row 39, col 34. UP didn't move anything (or did it?).
Actually, looking at the earlier evidence:
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39 (only left moved)
- Left at 19, right at 39: UP -> ???
I don't have evidence of what happened when left was at row 19 and UP was pressed. Let me check the history.
Actually, from the earlier turns:
- Left at 19, right at 39: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 44 (only right moved)
- Left at 19, right at 44: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 49 (only right moved)
- Left at 19, right at 49: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 54 (only right moved)
So when left was at row 19, DOWN moved the right square down. But I don't have evidence of UP from that state.
Now, with left at col 9, row 19, and right at col 44, row 39: UP moved the right square up. This suggests that the rule is:
- UP moves the square that is NOT blocked from moving up.
- If both are not blocked, both move.
- If both are blocked, nothing moves.
But earlier, when left was at col 19, row 19, and right was at col 34, row 39, UP didn't move the right square (based on the evidence that left moved from 24 to 19). Wait, let me re-check.
From the history:
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39
This means when left was at row 24 and right at row 39, UP moved left from 24 to 19. So left was not blocked at row 24, col 19, and it moved up to row 19. Right was not moved, which could mean right was blocked or the rule only moves one square.
But now, with left at row 19, col 9, and right at row 54, col 44, UP moved right from 54 to 49. So right was not blocked and moved up. Left was blocked at row 19, col 9, so it didn't move.
This suggests the rule is:
- UP: each square tries to move up. If not blocked, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
- DOWN: each square tries to move down. If not blocked, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
But this contradicts the earlier evidence where both squares moved together when at the same row. Let me re-examine.
From the history:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39 (both moved up)
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34 (both moved up)
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29 (both moved up)
When both are at the same row, both move up. This is consistent with the rule: each square tries to move up, and if not blocked, it moves.
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (only left moved)
When left is at row 29 and right at row 39, UP moves left from 29 to 24. Right stays at 39. This could mean right was blocked from moving up, or the rule only moves the higher square.
But now, with left at row 19, col 9, and right at row 54, col 44, UP moved right from 54 to 49. Left stayed at 19. This suggests left was blocked from moving up at col 9, row 19.
So the rule is: each square independently tries to move in the direction. If not blocked, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
This is a much simpler rule! Let me verify:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39. Both were not blocked, so both moved. ✓
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34. Both were not blocked, so both moved. ✓
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29. Both were not blocked, so both moved. ✓
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39. Left was not blocked (moved from 29 to 24). Right was blocked (stayed at 39). ✓
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39. Left was not blocked (moved from 24 to 19). Right was blocked (stayed at 39). ✓
- Left at 19, col 9, right at 54, col 44: UP -> left at 19, right at 49. Left was blocked (stayed at 19). Right was not blocked (moved from 54 to 49). ✓
This rule is consistent with all the evidence!
So the rule is:
- UP: each square tries to move up by 5. If not blocked by a wall, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
- DOWN: each square tries to move down by 5. If not blocked by a wall, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
- LEFT: left square tries to move left by 5, right square tries to move right by 5. If not blocked, they move. If blocked, they stay.
- RIGHT: left square tries to move right by 5, right square tries to move left by 5. If not blocked, they move. If blocked, they stay.
This is a much simpler model! Now I can plan more effectively.
Current state: left at (19, 9), right at (39, 44).
- Left can move: DOWN (from 19 to 24), RIGHT (from 9 to 14). UP is blocked (at row 19, col 9).
- Right can move: UP (from 39 to 34), DOWN (from 39 to 44), LEFT (from 44 to 49), RIGHT (from 44 to 39).
Wait, but earlier I observed that when both are at the same row, both move. Let me verify this with the new model.
If both are at row 44, and both try to move up:
- Left at row 44, col 19: tries to move to row 39. If not blocked, moves to 39.
- Right at row 44, col 34: tries to move to row 39. If not blocked, moves to 39.
Both move to row 39. ✓
If left is at row 29, col 19, and right is at row 39, col 34:
- Left tries to move to row 24. If not blocked, moves to 24.
- Right tries to move to row 34. If blocked, stays at 39.
Left moves to 24, right stays at 39. ✓
So the rule is: each square independently tries to move. If the target position is within the chamber (not blocked by a wall), it moves. Otherwise, it stays.
Now, the key question is: what is the goal? And what are the vertical bounds at each column?
From the evidence:
- Left square at col 9: row 19 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Let me check.
- Left square at col 19: row 19 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Yes, from 19 to 24, 29, 34, etc.
- Right square at col 34: row 39 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Yes, from 39 to 44, 49, 44, etc.
- Right square at col 44: row 39 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Yes.
Wait, but earlier when right was at col 34, row 39, UP didn't move it. This suggests row 39 is the top at col 34. And when right was at col 44, row 39, UP didn't move it. So row 39 is the top at col 44 as well.
But now, right is at col 44, row 39, and UP moved it from 54 to 49 to 44 to 39. So at col 44, the right square can be at rows 39, 44, 49, 54. And row 39 is the top (blocked from moving up).
Now, let me think about the goal. Maybe the goal is to move both squares to the same row and column, or to specific target positions.
Let me try to move both squares to the same row. Current: left at (19, 9), right at (39, 44).
To move left down: DOWN. Left at col 9, row 19. Can it move down to row 24?
To move right down: DOWN. Right at col 44, row 39. Can it move down to row 44?
If I press DOWN:
- Left tries to move from 19 to 24. If not blocked, moves to 24.
- Right tries to move from 39 to 44. If not blocked, moves to 44.
Let me try this!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key insight: Each square independently tries to move. If not blocked, it moves.
# Current: left at (19, 9), right at (39, 44)
# Let me explore the vertical bounds and try to get both to the same row.

# First, let me map out the vertical bounds by trying moves in each direction.
# Left at (19, 9): UP blocked. Let's try DOWN.
# Right at (39, 44): UP blocked (at row 39). Let's try DOWN.

# Try DOWN to see if both can move down
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 2x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 2x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 3x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 3x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 4x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 4x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 5x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 5x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 6x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 6x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 7x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 7x DOWN: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Each square independently tries to move up 5. If not blocked, moves.", "DOWN": "Each square independently tries to move down 5. If not blocked, moves.", "LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5. Independent blocking.", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5. Independent blocking."}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left: col 9, row 19 (top). Right: col 44, row 39 (top). Each square moves independently.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to find target config. Maybe align squares or reach specific positions.", "recent_findings": ["Each square moves independently when not blocked", "Left at col 9: row 19 is top", "Right at col 44: row 39 is top"], "open_questions": ["What are the vertical bounds at each column?", "What is the goal condition?"], "current_plan": "Explore vertical bounds by moving DOWN, then try SPACE at various configs.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE at initial config"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39
This means when left was at row 24 and right at row 39, UP moved left from 24 to 19. So left was not blocked at row 24, col 19, and it moved up to row 19. Right was not moved, which could mean right was blocked or the rule only moves one square.
But now, with left at row 19, col 9, and right at row 54, col 44, UP moved right from 54 to 49. So right was not blocked and moved up. Left was blocked at row 19, col 9, so it didn't move.
This suggests the rule is:
- UP: each square tries to move up. If not blocked, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
- DOWN: each square tries to move down. If not blocked, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
But this contradicts the earlier evidence where both squares moved together when at the same row. Let me re-examine.
From the history:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39 (both moved up)
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34 (both moved up)
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29 (both moved up)
When both are at the same row, both move up. This is consistent with the rule: each square tries to move up, and if not blocked, it moves.
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (only left moved)
When left is at row 29 and right at row 39, UP moves left from 29 to 24. Right stays at 39. This could mean right was blocked from moving up, or the rule only moves the higher square.
But now, with left at row 19, col 9, and right at row 54, col 44, UP moved right from 54 to 49. Left stayed at 19. This suggests left was blocked from moving up at col 9, row 19.
So the rule is: each square independently tries to move in the direction. If not blocked, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
This is a much simpler rule! Let me verify:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39. Both were not blocked, so both moved. ✓
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34. Both were not blocked, so both moved. ✓
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29. Both were not blocked, so both moved. ✓
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39. Left was not blocked (moved from 29 to 24). Right was blocked (stayed at 39). ✓
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39. Left was not blocked (moved from 24 to 19). Right was blocked (stayed at 39). ✓
- Left at 19, col 9, right at 54, col 44: UP -> left at 19, right at 49. Left was blocked (stayed at 19). Right was not blocked (moved from 54 to 49). ✓
This rule is consistent with all the evidence!
So the rule is:
- UP: each square tries to move up by 5. If not blocked by a wall, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
- DOWN: each square tries to move down by 5. If not blocked by a wall, it moves. If blocked, it stays.
- LEFT: left square tries to move left by 5, right square tries to move right by 5. If not blocked, they move. If blocked, they stay.
- RIGHT: left square tries to move right by 5, right square tries to move left by 5. If not blocked, they move. If blocked, they stay.
This is a much simpler model! Now I can plan more effectively.
Current state: left at (19, 9), right at (39, 44).
- Left can move: DOWN (from 19 to 24), RIGHT (from 9 to 14). UP is blocked (at row 19, col 9).
- Right can move: UP (from 39 to 34), DOWN (from 39 to 44), LEFT (from 44 to 49), RIGHT (from 44 to 39).
Wait, but earlier I observed that when both are at the same row, both move. Let me verify this with the new model.
If both are at row 44, and both try to move up:
- Left at row 44, col 19: tries to move to row 39. If not blocked, moves to 39.
- Right at row 44, col 34: tries to move to row 39. If not blocked, moves to 39.
Both move to row 39. ✓
If left is at row 29, col 19, and right is at row 39, col 34:
- Left tries to move to row 24. If not blocked, moves to 24.
- Right tries to move to row 34. If blocked, stays at 39.
Left moves to 24, right stays at 39. ✓
So the rule is: each square independently tries to move. If the target position is within the chamber (not blocked by a wall), it moves. Otherwise, it stays.
Now, the key question is: what is the goal? And what are the vertical bounds at each column?
From the evidence:
- Left square at col 9: row 19 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Let me check.
- Left square at col 19: row 19 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Yes, from 19 to 24, 29, 34, etc.
- Right square at col 34: row 39 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Yes, from 39 to 44, 49, 44, etc.
- Right square at col 44: row 39 is the top (blocked from moving up). Can it move down? Yes.
Wait, but earlier when right was at col 34, row 39, UP didn't move it. This suggests row 39 is the top at col 34. And when right was at col 44, row 39, UP didn't move it. So row 39 is the top at col 44 as well.
But now, right is at col 44, row 39, and UP moved it from 54 to 49 to 44 to 39. So at col 44, the right square can be at rows 39, 44, 49, 54. And row 39 is the top (blocked from moving up).
Now, let me think about the goal. Maybe the goal is to move both squares to the same row and column, or to specific target positions.
Let me try to move both squares to the same row. Current: left at (19, 9), right at (39, 44).
To move left down: DOWN. Left at col 9, row 19. Can it move down to row 24?
To move right down: DOWN. Right at col 44, row 39. Can it move down to row 44?
If I press DOWN:
- Left tries to move from 19 to 24. If not blocked, moves to 24.
- Right tries to move from 39 to 44. If not blocked, moves to 44.
Let me try this!
